prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 58-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: This morning I urinated and the toilet water was pink-red. There was no pain at all
(no burning, no back/flank pain). I urinated twice since and it looks normal now. I feel fine
otherwise.
- History: I used to smoke (quit 10 years ago, about 20 pack-years). I take lisinopril for blood
pressure.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): A single episode of painless pink/red urine is blood in the urine (gross hematuria). Even if it clears quickly and you feel well, it can come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. Causes range from benign (temporary bleeding, stone that passed, infection) to serious (especially in men over 50 and former smokers) such as bladder or kidney cancer. Because it was visible blood and painless, it should be evaluated promptly with a urine test and usually imaging and/or urology follow-up. Seek emergency care sooner if bleeding returns heavily, you can’t urinate, you pass clots, develop fever, severe flank/back pain, dizziness, or feel unwell.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Painless pink-red urine is blood in the urine (hematuria) until proven otherwise. Even if it happened once and then cleared, it can come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. Causes range from benign (temporary bleeding, infection, stones) to serious (especially in men over 50 and with a smoking history) such as bladder or kidney cancer. The lack of pain and your age/smoking history make it important to get evaluated promptly with a urinalysis and likely imaging and/or a urology referral. If you develop clots, inability to urinate, heavy bleeding, fever, new pain, dizziness, or weakness, that becomes urgent/emergent.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): A single episode of painless pink-red urine is most consistent with blood in the urine (hematuria). Even if it clears quickly and you feel well, painless visible blood can sometimes come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. In men over 50—especially with a past smoking history—this needs prompt medical evaluation to rule out important causes (including urinary tract cancers), as well as stones, infection, or prostate issues. Because you have no pain, fever, or ongoing heavy bleeding, this is less suggestive of an emergency like a severe infection or obstructing stone, but it still shouldn’t be ignored. Seek assessment soon and bring details (time, amount, any clots, meds).

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Pink-red urine is most often blood in the urine (hematuria). Even one painless episode can be important. Common causes include urinary tract infection, kidney stones, prostate issues, or (less commonly but importantly) urinary tract cancers (bladder/kidney). Your lack of pain and your age plus past smoking history increase the need to evaluate it, even though it went away and you feel well. Many serious causes can bleed intermittently. You should have a prompt medical assessment with a urine test (to confirm blood and check for infection), and depending on results, further evaluation (often imaging and/or cystoscopy).

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Pink-red urine is most often blood in the urine (gross hematuria). Even if it happens once, clears quickly, and causes no pain, it can come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. Causes include urinary infection, kidney stones, prostate enlargement, or (less commonly but importantly) urinary tract cancers. Your age (58) and past smoking history increase the importance of prompt evaluation for cancer, even without pain. Lisinopril is not a typical cause of red urine. Because you feel well and it stopped, this is not automatically an emergency, but it should be assessed soon with urinalysis and likely imaging and/or urology follow-up.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Pink-red urine is most often blood in the urine (gross hematuria). Even if it happens once, clears quickly, and causes no pain, it can come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. Causes range from benign (exercise, small stone, infection, prostate enlargement) to more serious, including urinary tract cancers. Your age (58) and past smoking increase the importance of prompt evaluation. Because you feel well and it stopped, this is not automatically an emergency, but it should not be watched at home without medical assessment. A clinician will usually confirm blood on urinalysis and consider urine culture and imaging and/or urology referral.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Painless pink-red urine is most consistent with blood in the urine (hematuria). Even if it happens once and then clears, it can come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. Causes range from benign (temporary bleeding, small stone, infection) to more serious, including urinary tract cancers. Your age (58) and past smoking increase the importance of prompt evaluation. Because you feel well and the bleeding stopped, this is not automatically an emergency, but it should not be waited on for weeks. A clinician will typically confirm blood on urinalysis and decide on further testing to look for the source.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Painless pink-red urine is blood in the urine (gross hematuria) until proven otherwise. Even if it happened once and then cleared, it can come from the bladder, kidneys, or prostate. Causes include urinary infection, kidney stones (often painful but not always), enlarged prostate, and—especially in men over 50 and people with a smoking history—urinary tract cancers. Because it was visible blood and you’re 58 with past smoking, this needs prompt medical evaluation (urinalysis, urine culture, kidney function tests, and usually imaging and a urology referral for cystoscopy). Seek emergency care sooner if you develop clots, trouble urinating, severe pain, fever, dizziness, or heavy recurrent bleeding.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Pink-red urine is most often blood in the urine (hematuria). Even one painless episode can be important. Causes range from temporary/benign (exercise, minor irritation, infection, stones) to more serious problems (bladder or kidney cancer, especially in men over 50 and people with a smoking history). The fact that it was painless and then cleared does not rule out a significant cause. Because you feel well and have no severe pain, fever, or trouble urinating, this is less likely to be an emergency, but it should be evaluated promptly with a urinalysis and follow-up testing if blood is confirmed.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Seeing pink-red urine even once can mean blood in the urine (hematuria). Because it was painless and visible, it’s important to take seriously. Causes range from benign (temporary irritation, small stone, infection) to more serious problems (bladder/kidney tumors). Your age (58) and past smoking history increase the need to rule out cancer even if it clears quickly and you feel well. Lisinopril doesn’t typically cause this. You should get prompt evaluation with a urine test and likely imaging and referral to urology if blood is confirmed.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%
